perturbation.original_text: In the event that Oceanic Time Warner Cable removes the above content and or terminates this agreement prior to December 31, 2006, Oceanic Time Warner Cable agrees to pay a cancellation fee of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000) per month multiplied by the number of months remain on the agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Oceanic Time Warner Cable removes the above content and or terminates this agreement prior to December 31, 2006, Oceanic Time Warner Cable agrees to pay a cancellation fee of Five Thousand Dollars ($5,000) and no further compensation.
perturbation.explanation: By stating a fixed cancellation fee of $5,000 regardless of the remaining months, the modified text potentially contradicts state laws regarding contract breaches and damages, which may require compensation proportional to the actual loss suffered.
perturbation.law_explanation: Hawaiian law typically allows for expectation damages in breach of contract cases, meaning the injured party should be compensated to the extent they are put in as good a position as if the contract had been fully performed. A fixed $5,000 fee, regardless of remaining months, may not adequately compensate Watchit if the actual losses due to early termination exceed that amount.
perturbation.justification: The changed text establishes a fixed cancellation fee of $5,000, regardless of the remaining months on the agreement. This contradicts the principle of expectation damages, where the injured party should be compensated to the extent they are put in as good a position as if the contract had been fully performed, as described in the Law Explanation. The original text compensates based on the number of months remaining in the contract at a rate of $5,000 per month, while the changed text only provides a fixed one-time payment. Since state laws typically allow for expectation damages in breach of contract cases, the fixed fee could be deemed insufficient if the actual losses due to early termination exceed $5,000.

perturbation.original_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable will provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the content provided by Watchit.
perturbation.changed_text: Ocean Time Warner Cable will provide Watchit with data on viewership to both Channel 777 and specifically to the content provided by Watchit. Ocean Time Warner Cable reserves the right to share Watchit's confidential information with third-party companies.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence allows for sharing confidential information with third parties, potentially violating trade secret laws if the viewership data contains proprietary information and lacks safeguards.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets from unauthorized disclosure. If the viewership data constitutes a trade secret (e.g., provides a competitive advantage) and is disclosed without consent or a legal privilege, it violates the UTSA, specifically section 482B-4 regarding misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hawaii Revised Statutes § 482B-4 (2022) - Damages. :: 2022 Hawaii Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Hawaii Rev. Stat. 2023 Hawaii Rev. Stat. 2022 Hawaii Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Hawaii Revised Statutes Title 26. Trade Regulation and Practice 482B. Trade Secrets 482B-4 Damages. Universal Citation: HI Rev Stat § 482B-4 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next §482B-4 Damages. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). [L 1989, c 221, pt of §1] Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Hawaii may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a clause allowing Ocean Time Warner Cable to share Watchit's confidential information with third-party companies. This directly contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of trade secret law, as viewership data could constitute a trade secret. Hawaii Revised Statutes § 482B-4 outlines damages for misappropriation, encompassing unauthorized disclosure. The added clause in the modified text creates a situation where such unauthorized disclosure is explicitly permitted, thereby undermining the protections offered by the UTSA and creating a clear inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement will be in effect until the end of 2006 and will be evaluated at that time.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement will be in effect until the end of 2006 and will automatically renew indefinitely at the end of each year.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text introduces an automatic renewal clause without specifying conditions for cancellation, potentially conflicting with contract law principles that require clear and conspicuous terms, especially for evergreen clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: An indefinite automatic renewal without clear cancellation terms could be deemed a deceptive practice under Hawaii Revised Statutes § 480-2, which prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce if the terms are not adequately disclosed and understood by both parties, giving one party an unfair advantage.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hawaii Revised Statutes § 480-2 (2022) - Unfair competition, practices, declared unlawful. :: 2022 Hawaii Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Hawaii Rev. Stat. 2023 Hawaii Rev. Stat. 2022 Hawaii Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Hawaii Revised Statutes Title 26. Trade Regulation and Practice 480. Monopolies; Restraint of Trade 480-2 Unfair competition, practices, declared unlawful. Universal Citation: HI Rev Stat § 480-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next §480-2 Unfair competition, practices, declared unlawful. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are unlawful. (b) In construing this section, the courts and the office of consumer protection shall give due consideration to the rules, regulations, and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts interpreting section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) No showing that the proceeding or suit would be in the public interest (as these terms are interpreted under section 5(b) of the Federal Trade Commission Act) is necessary in any action brought under this section. (d) No person other than a consumer, the attorney general or the director of the office of consumer protection may bring an action based upon unfair or deceptive acts or practices declared unlawful by this section. (e) Any person may bring an action based on unfair methods of competition declared unlawful by this section. [L 1965, c 129, pt of §1; Supp, §205A-1.1; HRS §480-2; am L 1987, c 274, §2; am L 1988, c 51, §1; am L 2002, c 229, §2] Law Journals and Reviews Misrepresentation and Deception Under Section 480-2 of the Hawaii Revised Statutes. 10 HBJ 69. Updating Unfair
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an automatic renewal clause without specifying conditions for cancellation, which can be considered an unfair or deceptive practice. Hawaii Revised Statutes § 480-2(a) states that "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are unlawful." The indefinite automatic renewal, without clear terms, could be deemed unfair, giving one party an unfair advantage if the terms are not adequately disclosed and understood, contradicting this statute. The added evergreen clause, without any form of cancelation, creates an Inconsistency that is a Legal Contradiction.
